verses.text: Then came all the tribes of Israel to David unto Hebron, and spake, saying, Behold, we are thy bone and thy flesh. Also in time past, when Saul was king over us, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel: and the LORD said to thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be a captain over Israel. Guidance: 'All the tribes of Israel' come to Hebron. Not a delegation — the full northern federation. The negotiation Abner began before his murder has continued without him, and the elders have arrived to complete it. They make three arguments. (1) Kinship: 'we are thy bone and thy flesh.' Shared ancestry; David is one of them, not a foreign claimant. (2) Past service: 'when Saul was king, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel.' They are remembering David's pre-fugitive military service under Saul (1 Sam 18:5, 13-16). Twenty years of estrangement has not erased their memory of him as their effective commander. (3) Theology: 'the LORD said to thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be a captain over Israel.' They are aware of the LORD's word about David — the same word Abner had finally invoked (3:18). The northern tribes have known about it for years; they are publicly acknowledging it now. The chapter is showing us how a divided nation reunifies: by remembering shared blood, shared service, and a shared word from God. None of the three arguments alone would have sufficed; together they constitute a moral case for unification that the tribes find irresistible.

Then came all the tribes of Israel to David unto Hebron, and spake, saying, Behold, we are thy bone and thy flesh. Also in time past, when Saul was king over us, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel: and the LORD said to thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be a captain over Israel. Shared ancestry; David is one of them, not a foreign claimant. (2) Past service: 'when Saul was king, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel.' They are remembering David's pre-fugitive military service under Saul (1 Sam 18:5, 13-16). Twenty years of estrangement has not erased their memory of him as their effective commander. (3) Theology: 'the LORD said to thee, Thou shalt feed my people Israel, and thou shalt be a captain over Israel.' They are aware of the LORD's word about David — the same word Abner had finally invoked (3:18). The northern tribes have known about it for years; they are publicly acknowledging it now. None of the three arguments alone would have sufficed; together they constitute a moral case for unification that the tribes find irresistible.

So all the elders of Israel came to the king to Hebron; and king David made a league with them in Hebron before the LORD: and they anointed David king over Israel. Guidance: The third anointing of David. (1) Private, by Samuel at Bethlehem, 1 Sam 16:13. (2) Tribal, by the men of Judah at Hebron, 2 Sam 2:4. (3) National, by the elders of all Israel at Hebron, this verse. The progression has been three decades long. David is now formally king of the unified kingdom — Judah and the ten northern tribes — that will hold together for the next seventy-two years (his forty plus Solomon's thirty-two), then break apart under Rehoboam. The note 'David made a league with them in Hebron before the LORD' is a formal covenant ceremony. David is not just a king by acclamation; he is a king by covenant — terms negotiated, the LORD invoked as witness, both sides bound. The Hebrew berit means a binding agreement, the same word used of the Sinai covenant. Israelite kingship under David is grounded in covenant accountability, not absolute monarchy. The verse will be the constitutional cornerstone of Davidic political theology for the rest of the Old Testament.

David was thirty years old when he began to reign, and he reigned forty years. In Hebron he reigned over Judah seven years and six months: and in Jerusalem he reigned thirty and three years over all Israel and Judah. Guidance: A chronological footnote that the narrator places now because David's reign-counting officially begins with his tribal accession at Hebron (chapter 2). Age 30 at the start, 40 years total reign — placing his death at 70. The forty-year reign is the standard biblical reign-length for a great king (Saul also reigned forty per Acts 13:21; Solomon reigns forty; the same number recurs for great judges). The split: 7.5 years at Hebron over Judah only; 33 at Jerusalem over the unified kingdom. The numbers underline how late the unification came — David spent 8 of his 40 years over only a fifth of the nation, then 33 years over the whole. Most of the great achievements of his reign — the conquest of Jerusalem, the bringing up of the ark, the building of his palace, the foreign wars, the establishment of the dynasty through the LORD's covenant with him in chapter 7 — will happen in the Jerusalem period. The Hebron years were preparation; the Jerusalem years are the substance.

David was thirty years old when he began to reign, and he reigned forty years. In Hebron he reigned over Judah seven years and six months: and in Jerusalem he reigned thirty and three years over all Israel and Judah. Guidance: A chronological footnote that the narrator places now because David's reign-counting officially begins with his tribal accession at Hebron (chapter 2). Age 30 at the start, 40 years total reign — placing his death at 70. The forty-year reign is the standard biblical reign-length for a great king (Saul also reigned forty per Acts 13:21; Solomon reigns forty; the same number recurs for great judges). The split: 7.5 years at Hebron over Judah only; 33 at Jerusalem over the unified kingdom. The numbers underline how late the unification came — David spent 8 of his 40 years over only a fifth of the nation, then 33 years over the whole. Most of the great achievements of his reign — the conquest of Jerusalem, the bringing up of the ark, the building of his palace, the foreign wars, the establishment of the dynasty through the LORD's covenant with him in chapter 7 — will happen in the Jerusalem period. The Hebron years were preparation; the Jerusalem years are the substance.

And the king and his men went to Jerusalem unto the Jebusites, the inhabitants of the land: which spake unto David, saying, Except thou take away the blind and the lame, thou shalt not come in hither: thinking, David cannot come in hither. Guidance: Jerusalem. The city is, at this moment, a Jebusite fortress — a Canaanite holdout that Israel had failed to take when the tribes divided the land (Judg 1:21). The Jebusite city was small (about 11 acres) but immensely defensible, sitting on a steep ridge between the Kidron and Tyropoeon valleys, with sheer cliffs on three sides and a single narrow approach. The Jebusite taunt — 'Except thou take away the blind and the lame, thou shalt not come in hither' — has multiple readings. The most natural is sarcastic: 'even our blind and lame could defend this place against you.' Their confidence is geographical; the natural fortress had stood for centuries unconquered. 'Thinking, David cannot come in hither.' The narrator gives us their inner certainty. David's move on the city is the first major military operation of the unified reign, and the Jebusites believe it is impossible. The chapter will show God's answer. David's choice of Jerusalem is politically inspired — it sits on the border between Judah and Benjamin, belonging fully to neither tribe, so neither north nor south can claim possession. The new capital will be a neutral ground, founded by David's own conquest, owing its existence to him personally. Israel's future political identity is about to be welded to the city David is about to take.

Nevertheless David took the strong hold of Zion: the same is the city of David. And David said on that day, Whosoever getteth up to the gutter, and smiteth the Jebusites, and the lame and the blind, that are hated of David's soul, he shall be chief and captain. Wherefore they said, The blind and the lame shall not come into the house. Guidance: 'David took the strong hold of Zion' — the southern ridge that includes the Gihon spring. The city's water supply was outside the walls; a vertical water-shaft (the 'Warren's Shaft,' archaeologically confirmed) connected the spring to the city. David's incentive offer: 'Whosoever getteth up to the gutter (tzinnor — water-shaft or pipeline)' — i.e., climb up through the city's underground water system — 'shall be chief and captain.' 1 Chronicles 11:6 tells us Joab took the challenge and won; the chronicler's parallel passage names Joab as the one who climbed up. The defensive blind spot was the city's water system: defenders could not be on the wall and at the spring access at the same time. The taunt about 'the blind and the lame' is now picked up by David — 'the lame and the blind, that are hated of David's soul' — David turns the Jebusite mockery back on them. The closing line — 'wherefore they said, The blind and the lame shall not come into the house' — became a proverbial rule in the temple period (the temple is 'the house'): physically disabled persons were generally barred from priestly service (Lev 21:18-20). The proverb was a memory of the Jerusalem conquest applied later to temple access. The verse is morally uncomfortable to modern readers because of the disability language; the original is more about turning enemy mockery into a war-cry than about excluding disabled persons. The temple practice was a separate development. The chapter's focus is the strategic victory and the city's renaming.

So David dwelt in the fort, and called it the city of David. And David built round about from Millo and inward. And David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him. Guidance: David moves in. He renames the captured Jebusite fortress 'the city of David' — Ir-David — the first time in Israelite history a city is named after a current king. The political signal is potent: this is not a tribal city, not a federation city, not a religious city; it is the king's personal city. The Davidic monarchy gets its own geographic basis. David then begins construction: 'from Millo and inward.' Millo is probably the stepped-stone supporting terrace on the eastern slope (the structure visible in modern archaeology) — a massive earthwork that allowed buildings to extend down the slope. David expands the city outward from this engineered platform. The final clause is the chapter's quiet refrain: 'David went on, and grew great, and the LORD God of hosts was with him.' Three short phrases, building. The Hebrew name 'LORD God of hosts' (yhwh elohei tseva'ot) — first appearing here in 2 Samuel — is the LORD as commander of the armies of heaven, the divine commander-in-chief. The chapter is establishing the theological grammar of David's reign: he grew great because the LORD was with him. The success is not credited to David's strategy but to God's presence. Every king of Israel who came after will be measured against this clause.

And Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David, and cedar trees, and carpenters, and masons: and they built David an house. And David perceived that the LORD had established him king over Israel, and that he had exalted his kingdom for his people Israel's sake. Guidance: Hiram of Tyre — the Phoenician king of the great commercial city on the Mediterranean coast — opens diplomatic relations with the new monarch by sending cedar logs, carpenters, and masons. Cedar from Lebanon was the most valued construction wood in the ancient Near East; Phoenician craftsmen were the best builders in the region. The gift is enormous and the relationship will last fifty years — Solomon will draw on the same Hiram (or his successor) for the temple in 1 Kings 5. David's palace ('an house') goes up at the top of the city. International recognition follows the political consolidation. Then the theological summary: 'David perceived that the LORD had established him king over Israel, and that he had exalted his kingdom for his people Israel's sake.' The verb 'perceived' (yada — knew) is the recognition of the deeper purpose. David sees that the kingdom is not for his own glory; it is 'for his people Israel's sake.' The throne exists to serve the nation. The chapter is showing us a king who has thought about why he is king and has arrived at the right answer. The Davidic ideal — the king-as-servant whose office is for the people's good — is being formulated in his own mind in this verse. Every later prophet will hold subsequent kings to this standard.

And David took him more concubines and wives out of Jerusalem, after he was come from Hebron: and there were yet sons and daughters born to David. And these be the names of those that were born unto him in Jerusalem; Shammuah, and Shobab, and Nathan, and Solomon, Ibhar also, and Elishua, and Nepheg, and Japhia, And Elishama, and Eliada, and Eliphalet. Guidance: A second roster of David's family, this time the Jerusalem-born sons. The number is now substantial — six wives at Hebron plus the new wives and concubines at Jerusalem, with eleven more named sons born here. The Jerusalem-born sons include Nathan (an ancestor of Christ's bloodline per Luke 3:31) and Solomon (Christ's bloodline per Matthew 1:6 and David's eventual successor). Many of the rest are listed nowhere else in scripture; they vanish into the genealogical background. The narrator presents the growing harem matter-of-factly. The text does not editorialize, but Deuteronomy 17:17's prohibition on the king 'multiplying wives' is being progressively violated. The wisdom seed planted in 2 Sam 3:2-5 grows here. The marital practice will produce, within fifteen years, the rape of Tamar by Amnon (ch 13), Absalom's rebellion (chs 15-18), and the succession crisis between Adonijah and Solomon (1 Kgs 1). The chapter is recording the consolidation of David's family power even as it plants the seed of the family chaos that consolidation will eventually produce. The chapter does not separate the achievements from the choices that will undermine them; it lets both sit in the same paragraph. Faithful historiography records the success and the seeds of the failure together.

But when the Philistines heard that they had anointed David king over Israel, all the Philistines came up to seek David; and David heard of it, and went down to the hold. The Philistines also came and spread themselves in the valley of Rephaim. Guidance: The Philistines, who had treated David as an asset against Saul (1 Sam 27-29), now realize their old vassal has become king of all Israel — a continental threat, not a buffer state. They mobilize. 'All the Philistines came up to seek David.' The full pentapolis confederation moves against him. They spread out in the Valley of Rephaim — the broad plain just southwest of Jerusalem, perfect chariot terrain. The Philistines pick the geography that favors their armored advantage. David, hearing of the muster, 'went down to the hold' — probably the stronghold of Adullam (1 Chr 11:15-19 parallels this) or back to Zion. He is moving from offensive (taking Jerusalem) to defensive (preparing for invasion). The chapter is showing us that the unification did not buy David peace; it accelerated the next war. Sometimes the very moment of consolidation provokes the next external threat. The new throne will be tested in its first months by a Philistine campaign designed to crush it before it consolidates.

And David enquired of the LORD, saying, Shall I go up to the Philistines? wilt thou deliver them into mine hand? And the LORD said unto David, Go up: for I will doubtless deliver the Philistines into thine hand. Guidance: The verb David has used through the wilderness years returns: 'David enquired of the LORD.' The discipline has not eroded with the crown. Two questions, the same pattern as before (1 Sam 30:8): shall I go? will you deliver? The LORD answers both: 'Go up: for I will doubtless deliver the Philistines into thine hand.' The Hebrew 'doubtless' (nathon ethen — the infinitive absolute) is the strongest possible promise. David is being told not just to go but that the LORD has already decided the outcome. The chapter is showing us that the inquire-of-the-LORD habit, which produced victory after victory in the wilderness, continues to produce victory after victory on the throne. Sometimes the practices that prepared us for the office are the same practices that sustain us in it. The temptation of power is to stop asking because one has begun to assume; David is, in this verse, refusing the temptation.

And David came to Baal-perazim, and David smote them there, and said, The LORD hath broken forth upon mine enemies before me, as the breach of waters. Therefore he called the name of that place Baal-perazim. And there they left their images, and David and his men burned them. Guidance: The battle at Baal-perazim. The location is the slope between Jerusalem and the Rephaim valley; the name will be assigned afterward to commemorate what God did. David's victory line is one of the great theological one-liners in scripture: 'The LORD hath broken forth upon mine enemies before me, as the breach of waters.' The image is of a dam breaking — water that had been pent up suddenly bursts through and sweeps everything before it. The Philistine line was a dam holding back God's purpose; the LORD broke it. 'Therefore he called the name of that place Baal-perazim' — 'master of breakings,' or 'the place where the LORD broke through.' The detail in v21 is precious: 'they left their images, and David and his men burned them.' The Philistines had brought their idols into battle, just as the Israelites had brought the ark to Aphek in 1 Sam 4 — and just as the ark had been captured then, now the Philistine gods are captured. The reversal is exact. David burns the idols rather than displaying them as trophies (Deut 7:25 commanded the burning of foreign images). The chapter is showing us a king who keeps the Torah even in the moment of victory; he could have kept the idols as spoil of war, but he obeys the Mosaic command and burns them. Right victory is followed by right disposal of the spoils.

And the Philistines came up yet again, and spread themselves in the valley of Rephaim. And when David enquired of the LORD, he said, Thou shalt not go up; but fetch a compass behind them, and come upon them over against the mulberry trees. Guidance: A second Philistine offensive on the same ground. They have learned nothing from Baal-perazim; or perhaps they think the previous defeat was a tactical anomaly that won't repeat. David inquires of the LORD a second time. The temptation of repeated success is to assume the same answer; if it worked once, do it again. David refuses the assumption. The LORD's answer is different: 'Thou shalt not go up; but fetch a compass behind them' — i.e., circle around to attack from behind — 'and come upon them over against the mulberry trees.' Different tactics for a similar-looking situation. The lesson is profound. God's instructions are not formulas; the same enemy at the same valley gets a different battle plan. David's continued inquiry — instead of repeating chapter-20's frontal assault — is what unlocks the new strategy. The chapter is teaching us that the practice of asking is its own discipline, separate from the answers it produces. Even when you got an answer last time, ask again this time; the new answer may be opposite.

David's continued inquiry — instead of repeating chapter-20's frontal assault — is what unlocks the new strategy. The chapter is teaching us that the practice of asking is its own discipline, separate from the answers it produces. Even when you got an answer last time, ask again this time; the new answer may be opposite.

And let it be, when thou hearest the sound of a going in the tops of the mulberry trees, that then thou shalt bestir thyself: for then shall the LORD go out before thee, to smite the host of the Philistines. And David did so, as the LORD had commanded him; and smote the Philistines from Geba until thou come to Gazer. Guidance: The signal for attack is auditory: 'when thou hearest the sound of a going in the tops of the mulberry trees.' Hebrew qol tse'adah — 'the sound of marching' or 'the sound of stepping.' Some commentators read this as a literal acoustic event (wind through the trees the LORD will time precisely); others as the divine angelic army's footfall, audible only with faith. Either way, the cue is that David is waiting for the LORD to act first. 'For then shall the LORD go out before thee, to smite the host of the Philistines.' The verse declares one of the great patterns of biblical warfare: the LORD goes first; the army of God's people follows. The chapter is showing us a king who has learned to wait for the LORD's movement rather than initiate his own. The result: 'David did so, as the LORD had commanded him; and smote the Philistines from Geba until thou come to Gazer.' Geba is north of Jerusalem; Gezer is fifteen miles west on the coastal plain. The pursuit is total — across the entire Israelite uplands all the way to the Philistine border. The Philistine threat is broken for a generation. The chapter ends with the new monarchy secured against its oldest external enemy by the obedience of a king who keeps asking. The pattern is set: the LORD God of hosts is with David, David inquires, the LORD answers, the LORD goes before, the king follows. The kingdom is now established. Tomorrow, chapter 6, David will bring the ark to Jerusalem and the chapter after, chapter 7, the LORD will make the covenant promise that defines all subsequent biblical theology. The throne is ready for the central religious act of David's reign.
